schools.summary: A further resurrection appearance after the apparent close (20:30-31), at the Sea of Galilee where Jesus had promised to meet them (Mk 14:28; Mt 28:10); the risen Christ ‘manifested himself’ now and then over the forty days (Acts 1:3).
Augustine: the Gospel seemed ended, yet John adds this seaside appearance; Calvin: the Evangelist still labours to prove the resurrection by the witness of seven disciples.

Seven disciples gathered (five named — Peter, Thomas, Nathanael, the sons of Zebedee [James and John, unnamed because John is the author]; the two others perhaps Philip and Andrew), waiting in Galilee between resurrection and Pentecost.

Peter’s return to his old trade in the interval before Pentecost — natural, not an abandonment of his calling; the fruitless night ordered by providence that the coming miracle might shine the brighter.
Calvin: Peter’s fishing is not inconsistent with his apostleship — he abstained from its exercise till clothed with power; the empty net teaches that without Christ they can do nothing (15:5).

At daybreak Jesus stands on the shore, appearing suddenly; the disciples knew him not — through the twilight and distance, and because ‘their eyes were holden’ (cf. Lk 24:16), as in the other appearances.

themes: Jesus on the shore at dawn
Christ near in the fruitless hour

schools.summary: ‘Children’ (paidia, ‘my boys,’ a tender diminutive used by Jesus only here, echoed by aged John in 1 John 2:18); the question ‘have ye any meat?’ expects and draws the confession of failure — ‘No.’
At Christ’s word, ‘Cast on the right side,’ the disciples obey though not knowing him, and the net is filled past their strength to draw (cf. Lk 5:6) — Jesus making himself known by the miracle.
Wesley: not only a proof of power but a kind supply and an emblem of the great ingathering of men they would have; Cyril and others read it of the church’s harvest by the Spirit.

The two apostles true to character: John the contemplative first perceives ‘It is the Lord’; Peter the active, hearing it, girds his fisher’s coat (ependytēs; ‘naked’ = stripped of his outer garment, 1 Sam 19:24) and plunges into the sea to reach him.
The other six, ‘not far from land’ (about 200 cubits / 100 yards), come in the boat ‘dragging the net’ too full to lift — an eyewitness’s exact memory of the scene; a contrast to Peter’s plunge.

On landing they see a ‘fire of coals’ (anthrakian, the word of 18:18) with fish and bread already laid — a fresh provision, perhaps miraculous, not of their own catching; the risen Lord has prepared their breakfast.
Though a meal is already prepared, Christ bids them bring of the fish ‘ye have now caught’ (by his direction, v.6) — their catch joined to his provision for the breakfast.
Calvin: the catch is not ascribed to their skill but to Christ’s blessing — as we call our daily bread ‘ours’ yet ask it as God’s gift; their labour honoured though the increase is his.
Peter, at Christ’s command, draws the net ashore (doing what all had failed to, v.6) with 153 great fish — the exact count of a remarkable draught — ‘yet was not the net broken’ (contrast Lk 5:6).
Much symbolism has been read into the number 153 (fathers and others), but the plain sense is the eyewitness’s exact tally; the unbroken net a figure of the gospel net that loses none.

themes: an hundred and fifty and three

schools.summary: ‘Come and dine’ = come and break your fast (aristēsate, the morning meal, v.4); the disciples, sure it was the Lord, durst not ask ‘Who art thou?’ — held in reverent, awe-filled silence.
Augustine/Chrysostom: Christ ate (Lk 24:43) not from need but to prove his resurrection real; Calvin: their silence was reverent awe, not doubt.

Jesus, as host and ‘head of the family,’ takes the bread and gives it to them with the fish (eating too, Lk 24:43) — the taking-and-giving echoing the Last Supper, the feeding (6:11), and Emmaus (Lk 24:30).
The ‘third time’ Jesus was manifested to the assembled disciples (after 20:19 and 20:26), counting only those to the company, not the appearances to Mary, the women, Emmaus, or Peter.
Calvin: all of one day counts as one manifestation; the appearances given ‘at intervals’ to confirm the resurrection; Augustine: with this third the Gospel is brought to a close.

The searching ‘lovest thou me more than these?’ (‘these’ = the other disciples, recalling Peter’s boast, Mk 14:29; or his nets) draws the first of a triple confession answering the triple denial; ‘Feed my lambs.’
Chrysostom: love to Christ is proved by care for the flock; Theophylact: the superintendence committed to Peter.
Calvin: Peter is restored to the apostolic rank his denial had forfeited — love owned, commission renewed, without upbraiding.

sides.position: more than the other disciples love me (Peter’s boast)
more than these things — the boat and nets

schools.summary: The second question drops ‘more than these,’ pressing simply on Peter’s love; the charge ‘Feed (shepherd, poimaine) my sheep’ means the fuller pastoral oversight — guiding, guarding, ruling the flock.
Calvin: the flock are Christ’s own sheep, who hear his voice and not a stranger’s (10:5, 27) — entrusted to the pastor who loves the Shepherd.

The third question (answering the third denial) grieves Peter; many note the shift from agapaō (first two) to Peter’s phileō, which Jesus now takes up — Peter, grieved, rests on Christ’s omniscience: ‘thou knowest all things.’
Calvin: Peter, not perceiving the purpose, thought himself accused; the threefold probing heals the threefold denial, drawing out a humbled love resting on the Lord’s knowledge, not his own boasts.

debates.question: Is the agapaō/phileō interchange in vv.15-17 deliberate?

sides.position: the distinction is preserved (higher love vs. friend-love)
the words are used interchangeably in John

schools.summary: The contrast of free youth and bound age foretells Peter’s martyrdom: ‘stretch forth thy hands… another shall gird thee’ describes crucifixion (the Roman custom); the threefold denier made a martyr.
Calvin: Christ arms Peter for the coming warfare, demanding invincible courage and firmness in bearing the cross; Chrysostom: a lesson to all in how to love unto death.

John’s comment (cf. 12:33; 18:32): the foretold death was a martyr’s, glorifying God (tradition: crucified at Rome, head downward); then ‘Follow me’ — literal and a summons to follow Christ to the cross.
Augustine: ‘Follow me’ to Peter alone marks his call to follow Christ in martyrdom; Calvin/Wesley: the saints glorify God chiefly by suffering, sealing the gospel with their blood.

Peter, rising to follow Christ, turns and sees John (‘the disciple whom Jesus loved,’ identified by his place at the supper, 13:25) following — the two long companions; the modest self-naming sets up Peter’s question (v.21).

Peter, having heard his own end foretold, abruptly asks about John’s future (‘what shall this man do?’ — perhaps ‘what death?’) — prompted by loving interest, but with a touch of curiosity that looks aside from his own path.

A gentle rebuke of curiosity (‘what is that to thee?’) and renewal of Peter’s call (‘follow thou me’); ‘till I come’ read variously (the fall of Jerusalem, the judgment, or not a violent death) — Christ’s sovereign right to dispose of each servant.
Calvin: the clause is hypothetical, not an assertion that John would not die (a transcribers’ slip); each disciple has his own appointed path.

debates.question: What is meant by ‘till I come’?

sides.position: the day of judgment / Christ’s coming
merely a hypothetical, not affirming John’s survival

schools.summary: A rumour that John would not die arose from misreading Christ’s words (confirmed by John’s long life); John carefully corrects it — Jesus said not ‘He shall not die’ but the conditional ‘If I will that he tarry.’
Calvin: the error sprang from the disciples’ misunderstanding, which John writes to correct; Wesley: John himself seems uncertain whether he should die.
A formal attestation: the Beloved Disciple (v.20) ‘wrote these things’ — John now declaring himself the eyewitness author, that greater weight attach to his record.
The plural ‘we know’ debated: the church’s endorsement appended (Wesley; cf. Rom 16:22), or John’s editorial plural / the apostolic witnesses (Robertson, Chrysostom, Calvin).

debates.question: Who speaks in ‘we know that his testimony is true’?

sides.position: John himself (editorial plural) / the apostolic company

schools.summary: A reverent hyperbole on the fulness of Christ’s unrecorded works (cf. 20:30); the softening ‘I suppose’ (oimai, first person) marks John as the writer; strongly attested though absent from Sinaiticus.
Calvin: John passed over more than he wrote, recording Christ’s public office; Henry: bless God that enough is written to direct faith and practice (20:31), the rest beyond all books.

themes: ‘the world… could not contain the books’
